participants.reasoning: The extracted description says only 12 participants were recruited and randomized, whereas the official record states 44 participants were included in the analysis, with broader eligibility criteria.
participants.extracted: Adults and older adults with grade III or IV pressure ulcers receiving care within NHS Leeds (acute hospitals, community nursing services, nursing homes, or residential homes) were screened for eligibility. Twelve participants were recruited and randomised equally to negative pressure wound therapy (n=6) or standard care (n=6).
participants.official:
44 participants: all included in the analysis
Inclusion criteria listed: pressure ulcer graded III or IV according to the European Pressure Ulcer Advisory Panel Grading System; must receive primary care via Leeds Primary Care Trust; ulcer should contain at least 80% viable tissue or have a very thin layer of slough (nonviable tissue) requiring no further debridement prior to use of NPWT
Exclusion criteria listed: the presence of unclear undermining in the pressure ulcer cavity precluding the use

interventions.reasoning: The extracted interventions describe NPWT versus standard care with multiple dressing options and flexible routine practice, whereas the official text specifies a fixed VAC regimen for one group and saline gauze care with debridement and measurements for the other, so the treatments do not match.
interventions.extracted: Participants were randomised to either negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT) or standard care (SC). NPWT used VAC Therapy Units and Systems (Kinetic Concepts Inc.) in accordance with manufacturer guidance, with the pressure ulcer filled with either VAC WhiteFoam or GranuFoam dressings; the treating nurse and patient determined treatment duration in line with routine practice, and other dressings or procedures could be used as needed. In the SC arm, participants received one of three non-silver dressings chosen by the treating nurse: spun hydrocolloid (fibrous hydrocolloid), foam, or alginate, with dressing-change frequency determined by standard practice. If allocated trial treatment could no longer be continued, participants moved to non-trial treatment at the discretion of the treating clinician and remained in follow-up.
interventions.official: Group A: the wound was closed using a Wound Care Kit that includes foam dressing (V.A.C. Granu Foam Silver), film drape, TRAC pad with tubing and a drainage canister. Steril foam material was placed inside the wound and was attached to the canister through tubing. The canister was attached to the Vacuum‐Assisted Closure device (V.A.C.® Therapy System Patient Support – KCI), which is a portable device that applies intermittent or continuous negative pressure. The device was operated at 125 mmHg p

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes include many feasibility and clinical measures beyond the official primary and secondary outcomes, which only list complete wound healing and adverse events.
outcomes.extracted: The pilot trial planned to measure feasibility and clinical outcomes including recruitment rate (number of participants with grade III/IV pressure ulcers recruited from acute and primary care over the 12-month recruitment period); participant follow-up duration/attrition (time from randomisation to trial exit, with reasons for attrition recorded up to a maximum of 6 months); hospital admission and discharge events and discharge destinations during follow-up; pressure ulcer healing/time to healing (date of complete healing of the reference ulcer, defined as epithelialisation and cessation of treatment, assessed during follow-up up to 6 months, with monthly photographs and additional weekly photographs for 4 weeks after healing); blinded photographic assessment of healing and treatment allocation using digital wound photographs at baseline, monthly during follow-up, and at healing; ulcer-related pain measured at baseline and follow-up using a 10-cm visual analogue scale; health-related quality of life measured at baseline and follow-up using EQ-5D; treatment use including frequency of dressing changes, duration of allocated treatment, and reasons for treatment change/cessation, recorded at each treatment visit during follow-up; and adverse events, including all non-serious adverse events plus serious adverse events such as death, life-threatening events, hospital admission/prolongation, disability/incapacity, systemic infection/sepsis, osteomyelitis, and ulcer closure surgery, recorded throughout follow-up.
outcomes.official:
Primary outcomes: complete wound healing (% ulcers healed)
Secondary outcomes: adverse events
